[00:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Hi, I'm Dr Patel. Thanks for waiting. You're here with Milo today, right?

[00:06] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Yes, Milo, he's four, Labrador, male neutered. He's had diarrhoea since yesterday morning.

[00:15] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Okay, tell me from the beginning. When did you first notice it and how many episodes?

[00:24] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** First one was about 6am yesterday. Then maybe six or seven times yesterday, and three times this morning before we came.

[00:36] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Got it. What's the stool like? Fully watery, soft, mucus, any blood?

[00:44] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Mostly watery with some mucus. I saw a tiny streak of red once last night, then none after that.

[00:55] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any vomiting?

[00:58] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** One vomit yesterday afternoon, yellow foamy stuff. None overnight.

[01:05] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Appetite and drinking?

[01:08] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Appetite lower. He ignored breakfast today, which is unusual. He is still drinking, maybe a bit more than normal.

[01:19] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Energy level?

[01:22] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Slightly quieter but still waggy and wanting to go out. Not collapsed or anything.

[01:30] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any straining, urgency, accidents in the house?

[01:35] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Yes, urgency. He asked to go out at 2am. No house accident, just barely made it outside.

[01:44] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any cough, sneeze, breathing change, or urinary issues?

[01:49] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** No cough or sneeze. Peeing seems normal.

[01:56] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Has he eaten anything unusual? New treats, bin raiding, park scavenging?

[02:03] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** We were in the park Saturday and there was a picnic. He grabbed some sausage and maybe a bit of bread before I could stop him.

[02:15] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any cooked bones, fatty leftovers, grapes, chocolate, onions, xylitol?

[02:23] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** No chocolate or onions. Definitely no grapes this time. He did have a grape incident a year ago but he was fine after treatment.

[02:35] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Thanks, that's useful. Any travel, kennel stay, or contact with dogs who had tummy bugs?

[02:42] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** No travel. He goes to daycare once a week, and one dog there had loose stools last week.

[02:52] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Current medications or supplements?

[02:55] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Nothing regular. I gave Pro-Kolin paste at home, about 2 ml twice yesterday, but I guessed the amount.

[03:06] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any flea/worm treatment recently?

[03:10] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Wormed about six weeks ago. Flea treatment three weeks ago.

[03:17] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any previous GI disease, pancreatitis, food allergy diagnosis?

[03:22] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** No formal diagnosis. He had mild diarrhoea once after stealing cat food, resolved in two days.

[03:31] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Is he up to date with vaccinations?

[03:34] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Yes, all up to date. Last booster was around four months ago.

[03:41] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Great. Any chance he got into household chemicals or garden products?

[03:47] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Not that I know of. We keep that stuff in a locked cupboard.

[03:54] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any known drug allergies?

[03:57] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** None known.

[04:02] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** I'll do a full exam now. We'll check hydration, tummy comfort, temp, heart, lungs, and gums.

[04:11] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Sure.

[04:16] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Milo's bright and responsive, a bit quieter than typical but still friendly. Weight today is 28.4 kg.

[04:27] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Temp is 38.7°C. HR 108, RR 28. Mucous membranes pink and moist, CRT under 2 seconds.

[04:40] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Mild abdominal discomfort on deep cranial palpation, no obvious mass, no severe guarding.

[04:50] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Lungs clear, heart sounds normal rhythm, peripheral pulses good quality.

[04:58] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Hydration looks mildly reduced, around 5%, mainly from tackier gums and history.

[05:06] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Okay, so he's not dangerously dehydrated?

[05:10] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Not at this stage, no. He's stable for outpatient treatment today.

[05:18] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Based on history and exam, this is most consistent with acute gastroenteritis, likely dietary indiscretion.

[05:29] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** I'm not seeing signs right now that force immediate hospitalisation, but we should monitor closely over 24–48 hours.

[05:39] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** That's a relief. I was worried it might be something severe.

[05:44] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Fair concern. Severe concerns would be persistent vomiting, blood-heavy diarrhoea, collapse, marked lethargy, or worsening pain.

[05:56] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Understood.

[06:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** For treatment, I recommend an anti-nausea injection now: maropitant 1 mg/kg SC in clinic.

[06:10] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Then at home: Pro-Kolin 5 ml by mouth every 8 hours for 3 days, and a bland diet in small frequent meals.

[06:22] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** 5 ml every 8 hours, okay. He can be fussy with tablets.

[06:27] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** That's fine; this is paste, usually easier than tablets. If he spits it out, place small amounts at the side of the mouth.

[06:37] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Perfect. How long bland diet?

[06:40] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Start 3 days, then gradually transition to normal food over 2–3 days if stools improve.

[06:49] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Water intake okay as normal?

[06:52] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Yes, encourage water, small frequent drinks are good. Avoid high-fat treats and no new foods this week.

[07:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** We can discuss stool testing if he doesn't improve in 48 hours, or sooner if signs worsen.

[07:08] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Should we do stool PCR now?

[07:11] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Optional today. Given he's stable and this is short duration, many cases resolve with supportive care. If persistent, then faecal panel is sensible.

[07:23] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Let's hold for now and monitor.

[07:26] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Agreed.

[07:30] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any insurance constraints I should know about?

[07:34] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** We have insurance but prefer to keep first step simple if safe.

[07:39] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** That's reasonable with current clinical picture.

[07:44] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Sorry, he also ate a sock once last year, but he passed it.

[07:49] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Thanks. Any concern he swallowed fabric this week?

[07:53] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** No, not this week.

[07:56] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Okay.

[08:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Let me just repeat key red flags so we're aligned.

[08:05] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Please call urgently if: repeated vomiting, unable to keep water down, blood increasing in stool, marked lethargy, abdominal pain, or no improvement by 48 hours.

[08:18] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Yes, I'll watch for those.

[08:22] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** For admin and follow-up, I'd like a nurse call tomorrow afternoon to check progress.

[08:29] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If still loose by then, we'll book recheck and likely stool test.

[08:35] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Tomorrow afternoon works.

[08:38] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Great. Reception can schedule that before you leave.

[08:44] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Is probiotic yogurt okay?

[08:47] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Better to avoid extras for now; stick to prescribed GI support and bland diet to keep variables controlled.

[08:56] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Makes sense.

[09:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any concerns giving paste three times daily?

[09:04] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Morning and evening easy. Midday is hard when I'm at work.

[09:09] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If midday impossible, do best practical schedule and keep me updated; ideally q8h, but partial adherence is still better than none.

[09:19] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Okay, I can ask my partner to do lunchtime dose.

[09:24] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Good plan. Also, short lead walks only today, mainly for toileting.

[09:30] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** He'll hate that, but okay.

[09:34] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** I know. Labs love chaos.

[09:37] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** He really does. He stole a sandwich in Hyde Park last month.

[09:42] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** That tracks perfectly with this breed.

[09:46] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** I'll have nurse Sarah give the maropitant injection now and dispense Pro-Kolin with printed instructions.

[09:54] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Great, thank you.

[09:58] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Before we finish, quick recap:

[10:01] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Acute diarrhoea, one vomit, mild dehydration, stable exam, likely acute gastroenteritis/dietary indiscretion.

[10:11] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Plan is antiemetic injection today, Pro-Kolin 5 ml PO q8h x3 days, bland diet, monitor red flags, nurse follow-up tomorrow.

[10:21] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Clear, thank you.

[10:25] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Should I send stool photos by WhatsApp?

[10:29] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Yes, that can help if anything changes. Include timestamp and whether he ate beforehand.

[10:36] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** He also has a grooming appointment next week, can he still go?

[10:41] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If fully recovered, yes. If still GI signs, better postpone.

[10:47] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Is chicken and rice definitely okay?

[10:50] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Yes, small frequent portions. If he refuses chicken/rice, use a commercial GI diet instead.

[10:58] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** We can do that.

[11:02] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any concern about worms despite recent treatment?

[11:06] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Not sure, he sniffs fox poo constantly.

[11:10] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If diarrhoea persists, we'll include parasite testing and consider targeted treatment based on results.

[11:18] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Okay.

[11:20] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any other questions before I bring nurse in?

[11:24] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** No, that covers it.

[11:28] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Perfect. I'll document this now.

[11:31] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Thanks, doctor.

[11:36] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** (to nurse, in room) Sarah, please do maropitant 1 mg/kg SC for Milo, 28.4 kg, then dispense Pro-Kolin with q8h instructions.

[11:48] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Also please ask reception to set a nurse progress call for tomorrow afternoon.

[11:55] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Thank you both.

[12:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** While Sarah prepares that, one final thing: if he becomes very lethargic overnight, call emergency immediately.

[12:09] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** I will.

[12:12] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Great. We'll check in tomorrow and decide if stool diagnostics are needed.

[12:18] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Sounds good.

[12:22] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Has he passed stool since arriving?

[12:25] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Not yet.

[12:27] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If he does, we can provide a pot and sample instructions in case we need testing quickly.

[12:34] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Yes, please give me a pot just in case.

[12:38] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Will do.

[12:42] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Do you want us to email the care plan as well?

[12:46] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Email please, easier to share with my partner.

[12:50] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Noted.

[12:54] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Sorry, one more random thing, he's due nail trim.

[12:58] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Let's defer non-urgent handling today so we keep stress low.

[13:03] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Fair enough.

[13:06] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Recap dose again: Pro-Kolin 5 ml, every 8 hours, for 3 days.

[13:13] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** 5 ml q8h x3 days, got it.

[13:17] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Perfect.

[13:20] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If stool normalises sooner, still complete the planned course.

[13:25] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Okay.

[13:27] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** If no improvement by 48 hours, call us; we'll recheck and run faecal testing.

[13:33] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Understood.

[13:36] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Any current meds at all, including human supplements, fish oils, herbal products?

[13:42] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** None.

[13:44] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Great.

[13:47] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** We'll avoid empirical antibiotics unless clinical picture changes.

[13:52] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** I'm happy with that.

[13:55] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Nice. I'll hand over now.

[13:58] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Thank you.

[14:02] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** (after injection) Injection done, he tolerated it well.

[14:07] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Brave boy, yes.

[14:10] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Reception has booked nurse phone update for tomorrow at 15:30.

[14:16] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Perfect.

[14:19] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** You'll get email instructions and a stool pot on checkout.

[14:24] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Great, thanks.

[14:27] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Safe trip home. Call anytime if worried.

[14:31] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Will do, thanks again.

[14:35] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Bye Milo.

[14:37] **Speaker 2 (Owner):** Bye.

[14:40] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** (end of consult notes spoken) Diagnosis discussed as likely acute gastroenteritis/dietary indiscretion; no evidence today for severe systemic compromise.

[14:50] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** Outpatient management agreed. Red flags and follow-up clearly communicated to owner.

[15:00] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** (admin handover) Tasks: nurse follow-up call tomorrow 15:30; provide stool pot; email care plan; reception to tag case for 48h review if no improvement.

[15:11] **Speaker 1 (Vet):** End recording.
